summary: Martin Fowler shares his experience upgrading his home media server to an HP N36L. The upgrade was motivated by two main factors: switching from IDE to SATA drives for easier maintenance, and reducing power consumption from 80W to 20W. He installed Ubuntu 10.04 LTS and experimented with Puppet for configuration management. The post is a brief, practical hardware recommendation for home server enthusiasts.
key_points: The HP N36L offers easy hot-swappable SATA drive access through a front door design
Power consumption dropped dramatically from 80W to 20W idle, though ROI at $6/month savings is minimal
The compact, quiet form factor suits various home environments
Using Puppet for a home server is overkill but provides useful DevOps learning experience
money_quotes: At $6 a month, I'm not going to be making a great ROI on this, but at least I'll feel more virtuous about leaving it on all the time.
I also enjoyed the humor of the vendor in including a 160GB drive in the package. (I put in something rather larger.)
I took the opportunity to use puppet - overkill for a home server but useful to give me some devops creds.
Overall something worth recommending but nothing too dramatic - but then I don't like drama in a server.
key_insight: A good home server should be boring and reliable—dramatic improvements in power efficiency and maintainability matter more than flashy features.
